content.text: Model Config Promotion Across Environments

Model configuration — the model ID, temperature, max_tokens, response format, and prompt version — must be identical from staging to production. If staging runs gpt-4o-mini at temperature 0.4 and production runs gpt-4o at temperature 0.2, every eval result from staging is measuring a different system than what users experience. The validation is meaningless.

Promotion discipline means treating model config as a typed, versioned artifact that flows through the same promotion pipeline as the application image: authored in Git, validated by CI, deployed to staging, evaluated, then promoted to production with an explicit approval gate.

attrs.alt: Model config promotion pipeline across dev, staging, and production environments

content.text: Config File Structure

Keep model configuration in a separate YAML file per environment under a versioned config directory. The staging and production files should differ only on infrastructure parameters (replica count, rate limits) — never on model identity or generation parameters.

# config/model-config.staging.yaml
model_configs:
  rag_answer:
    model: gpt-4o-2024-05-13        # exact versioned snapshot, not 'gpt-4o'
    temperature: 0.2
    max_tokens: 1024
    top_p: 0.95
    response_format: json_object
    prompt_version: v2
    timeout_seconds: 30

  summarization:
    model: gpt-4o-2024-05-13
    temperature: 0.3
    max_tokens: 512
    response_format: text
    prompt_version: v1
    timeout_seconds: 20

# Infrastructure params (allowed to differ from prod)
rate_limit_per_minute: 100
replica_count: 2
log_level: debug

# config/model-config.prod.yaml
model_configs:
  rag_answer:
    model: gpt-4o-2024-05-13        # MUST match staging exactly
    temperature: 0.2                 # MUST match staging exactly
    max_tokens: 1024                 # MUST match staging exactly
    top_p: 0.95                      # MUST match staging exactly
    response_format: json_object     # MUST match staging exactly
    prompt_version: v2               # MUST match staging exactly
    timeout_seconds: 30

  summarization:
    model: gpt-4o-2024-05-13
    temperature: 0.3
    max_tokens: 512
    response_format: text
    prompt_version: v1
    timeout_seconds: 20

# Infrastructure params (intentional differences from staging)
rate_limit_per_minute: 500
replica_count: 8
log_level: info
CI Parity Check

A CI job runs on every PR that modifies either config file and enforces that the model generation parameters are identical across staging and production. It fails on any mismatch in the fields that affect model behavior.
Pinning Model Snapshot Versions

Model providers update their model snapshots without notice. `gpt-4o` today is not the same model as `gpt-4o` next month — providers silently roll out updated weights under the same alias. Always pin to a dated snapshot ID in model config to get reproducible behavior across builds and evaluations.
Alias (avoid)
Pinned snapshot (use)
Notes
gpt-4o
OpenAI dated snapshot, stable for 6–12 months
gpt-4o-mini
Updated independently from gpt-4o
claude-3-5-sonnet
claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022
Anthropic uses date suffix for pinning
claude-3-haiku
claude-3-haiku-20240307
Older but stable for cost-sensitive workloads
gemini-1.5-pro
gemini-1.5-pro-001
Google uses numeric suffix; check availability by region

Treat a model snapshot upgrade the same as a prompt version upgrade: write an eval comparison, run the regression suite, confirm pass rate is equal or better, then open a PR updating the snapshot ID in config.
Promotion Approval Workflow

Config promotion from staging to production should require explicit human approval in addition to CI green. The approval step creates an audit record and forces a human to acknowledge what is changing.

# .github/workflows/promote-model-config.yml
name: Promote Model Config to Production

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      feature:
        description: 'Feature to promote (e.g. rag_answer, or "all")'
        required: true
        default: 'all'
      reason:
        description: 'Reason for promotion (audit trail)'
        required: true

jobs:
  validate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Run parity check
        run: python3 scripts/check_model_config_parity.py

      - name: Run staging regression eval
        run: |
          python3 scripts/run_regression_eval.py \
            --env staging \
            --feature ${{ github.event.inputs.feature }} \
            --min-pass-rate 0.90

  promote:
    needs: validate
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production   # requires manual approval in GitHub Environments
    steps:
      - name: Deploy prod config
        run: |
          kubectl apply -f k8s/genai-service/configmap-prod.yaml \
            --namespace genai-prod

      - name: Write audit record
        run: |
          python3 scripts/write_config_promotion_audit.py \
            --feature "${{ github.event.inputs.feature }}" \
            --reason "${{ github.event.inputs.reason }}" \
            --actor "${{ github.actor }}" \
            --run-id "${{ github.run_id }}"

# scripts/write_config_promotion_audit.py
import argparse, datetime, json, boto3, os

def write_audit(feature: str, reason: str, actor: str, run_id: str) -> None:
    record = {
        "ts": datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z",
        "event": "model_config_promoted",
        "feature": feature,
        "reason": reason,
        "actor": actor,
        "github_run_id": run_id,
        "staging_config_sha": os.environ.get("STAGING_CONFIG_SHA", "unknown"),
        "prod_config_sha": os.environ.get("PROD_CONFIG_SHA", "unknown"),
    }
    s3 = boto3.client("s3")
    key = f"model-config-audit/{record['ts']}-{feature}.json"
    s3.put_object(Bucket="genai-audit-log", Key=key,
                  Body=json.dumps(record, indent=2),
                  ContentType="application/json")
    print(f"Audit record written: s3://genai-audit-log/{key}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    p = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    p.add_argument("--feature"); p.add_argument("--reason")
    p.add_argument("--actor"); p.add_argument("--run-id")
    args = p.parse_args()
    write_audit(args.feature, args.reason, args.actor, args.run_id)
Loading Config in Application Code

Application code should load model config at startup from a single source of truth — never hardcode model IDs or generation params inline. This makes config changes deployable without code changes and ensures the running config is always observable.
Observing Active Config in Production

Expose the active model config as a health endpoint so it is always observable without reading files or environment variables. This is essential for post-incident debugging — confirm exactly what model and params were running at the time of an incident.

The /config endpoint output from staging and production should be identical except for the env field. If they differ, a parity violation slipped through — use this endpoint in integration tests to catch it before users do.
Summary
Model ID, temperature, max_tokens, response_format, and prompt_version must be identical from staging to production — differences make staging evals worthless.
Store config in per-environment YAML files; enforce parity on inference fields with a CI script that fails on any mismatch.
Always pin to dated model snapshot IDs (gpt-4o-2024-05-13, claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022) — floating aliases silently update and invalidate your eval baselines.
Treat a snapshot ID upgrade the same as a prompt change: run the regression eval suite and require a PR with documented rationale.
Gate production promotion behind a GitHub Environment requiring manual approval; write every promotion to an append-only audit log with the approver identity and reason.
Dev environments may use cheaper or faster models for iteration speed — but staging must use the production model to generate valid pre-flight signal.
